options.text: The vertical axis starts at $5000$, not $0$, so a small difference looks huge.
Belford really does have several times more people than Ashby.
options.why: Reads the bar heights as true; the values $5680$ and $5280$ are actually very close.

options.text: The bars are drawn with different widths.
options.why: Names the wrong feature; the three bars here have equal widths.

options.text: There are no numbers on the vertical axis.
options.why: The axis is labelled $5000$ to $5700$; the problem is where it starts, not missing labels.

questions.solution_text: The vertical axis starts at $5000$, not $0$. That cut-off stretches a small gap ($5280$ to $5680$) into a huge-looking difference.

options.text: Shop B's bar is much wider, so its area exaggerates a small difference.
Shop B sold about three times as many items as Shop A.
options.why: Trusts the apparent size; the values $550$ and $500$ are close, not triple.

options.text: The vertical axis does not start at $0$.
options.why: The axis here does start at $0$; the distortion is the unequal bar widths.

options.text: The bars are in different colours.
options.why: Both bars are the same colour, and colour would not distort the values.

questions.solution_text: Shop B's bar is drawn much wider than Shop A's, so its larger area makes it look far bigger, though it sold only slightly more.

options.text: The symbol is doubled in both width and height, so its area is four times as big.
The symbols are placed too far apart.
options.why: The spacing between symbols does not change how large each value appears.

options.text: The value labels $100$ and $200$ are wrong.
options.why: The labels are correct; the distortion is in the symbol sizes, not the numbers.

options.text: The symbols should be circles, not squares.
options.why: The shape is not the issue; scaling both dimensions is what exaggerates the area.

questions.solution_text: Doubling the value doubled both the width and the height of the symbol. That makes its area $2\times2=4$ times as large, so the rise looks like four times, not double.

options.text: The years are unequally spaced in time but drawn with equal gaps.
The vertical axis does not start at $0$.
options.why: The vertical axis does start at $0$; the fault is the uneven spacing along the time axis.

options.text: A line graph should never be used for sales.
options.why: A line graph is fine for change over time; the issue is the unequal year spacing.

options.text: The sales values are too small to plot.
options.why: The values plot clearly; magnitude is not what distorts the trend.

questions.solution_text: The gap from $2015$ to $2019$ is four years but is drawn the same width as the one-year gap before it. Equal spacing of unequal time gaps makes the rate of increase look steady.

options.text: The percentages add to $160\%$, so they cannot be shares of one whole.
Maths clearly has more than half of all the A grades.
options.why: Treats $60\%$ as valid, but the shares total more than $100\%$, so they are not reliable.

options.text: The bars have different widths.
options.why: The bars have equal widths; the fault is that the percentages exceed $100\%$.

options.text: The vertical axis starts above $0$.
options.why: The axis starts at $0$; the problem is the impossible total, not a truncated scale.

questions.solution_text: The three shares are $60\%$, $55\%$ and $45\%$, which add to $160\%$. Shares of a single whole must total $100\%$, so this data cannot be a fair breakdown of the A grades.

options.text: No — the axis starts at $0$ with equal steps and equal bar widths, so it is fair.
Yes — the axis starts at $0$, which hides the differences.
options.why: Starting at $0$ is the fair choice; it shows the true sizes rather than hiding them.

options.text: Yes — the bars are almost the same height, so data is missing.
options.why: The near-equal heights are honest because the three populations really are close.

options.text: Yes — a bar graph cannot be used for populations.
options.why: A bar graph is an appropriate display for comparing amounts across towns.

questions.solution_text: It is not misleading. The axis starts at $0$ and rises in equal steps, and the bars are equally wide. The bars are almost level, which truthfully shows the three populations are close.

options.text: The axis starts at $30$ (not $0$) and the bars have unequal widths.
The axis starts at $0$ and the bars are equally wide.
options.why: Describes a fair graph; here the axis starts at $30$ and the widths are unequal.

options.text: The percentages add to more than $100\%$ and there is no title.
options.why: There are no percentages and a title is present; these are not the distortions shown.

options.text: The colours differ and the years are unequally spaced.
options.why: There are no years on this graph, and the bar colour is the same for both stores.

questions.solution_text: Two features distort it. First, the axis starts at $30$, not $0$, stretching the gap between $40$ and $44$. Second, Store B's bar is much wider, so its area exaggerates the small difference.
